clips.title: 穿着巧克力衬衫的70岁老人

lines.en: Brad is 70, retired in West Palm Beach, Florida.
lines.zh: 布拉德今年70岁，退休后住在佛罗里达州西棕榈滩。

lines.en: And when we first talked to him, he showed up wearing a bright orange, I guess, Hawaiian shirt, but with a bunch of Reese's peanut butter cups all over it.
lines.zh: 我们第一次和他聊的时候，他穿着一件亮橙色的——我猜算是夏威夷衫吧——上面还印满了Reese's花生酱杯。

lines.en: Honestly, pretty snazzy.
lines.zh: 说实话，还挺拉风的。

lines.en: I mean, I guess you could call me a big Reese's fan.
lines.zh: 我的意思是，你也可以说我是个超级Reese's粉丝。

lines.en: And it's his love of Reese's that is keeping him from his beloved karaoke right now.
lines.zh: 而正是他对Reese's的热爱，让他现在没法去唱他最爱的卡拉OK。

lines.en: Yeah, because a little while ago, he heard that the Hershey Company had released a new Reese's chocolate.
lines.zh: 对，因为前阵子他听说好时公司推出了一款新的Reese's巧克力。

lines.en: It was Reese's peanut butter mini hearts unwrapped.
lines.zh: 那款产品叫Reese's迷你花生酱爱心巧克力（独立无包装版）。

lines.en: OK, and of course, then I went out and bought one, bought a pouch.
lines.zh: 然后我当然就跑去买了一包。

lines.en: Brad opens the bag and pops some mini hearts in his mouth.
lines.zh: 布拉德打开袋子，往嘴里丢了几颗迷你爱心巧克力。

lines.en: And I took two bites and it was not recognizable.
lines.zh: 我咬了两口，就发现这味道完全不对。

lines.en: It was just nasty.
lines.zh: 那东西简直难吃死了。

lines.en: It was not edible.
lines.zh: 根本没法吃。

lines.en: Brad spits out the mini hearts and then he dumps them in the trash.
lines.zh: 布拉德把那些迷你爱心巧克力吐了出来，然后直接扔进了垃圾桶。

lines.en: But then he's like, wait, what was so off about those chocolate hearts?
lines.zh: 但接着他又说，等等，那些巧克力爱心到底哪里不对劲？

lines.en: I retrieved the pouch wrapper and looked carefully at the front and the back.
lines.zh: 我把那个小袋子的包装捡了回来，仔细看了看正面和背面。

lines.en: And there was no milk chocolate and there was no real peanut butter.
lines.zh: 结果里面既没有牛奶巧克力，也没有真正的花生酱。

lines.en: So it wasn't even, I don't know what I, Greg, I have no idea what I was eating or what I was tasting.
lines.zh: 所以那甚至都不算——我都不知道该怎么说，Greg，我完全不知道自己刚才吃的、尝到的到底是什么。

lines.en: Brad is like, what is even in here?
lines.zh: 布拉德就说，这里面到底装的是什么啊？

lines.en: No milk chocolate, no peanut butter in a chocolate Reese's peanut butter cop light product?
lines.zh: 一个仿瑞斯花生酱杯的巧克力产品，居然没有牛奶巧克力，也没有花生酱？

clips.title: 她用鼻子诊断了一种病

lines.en: Les and I met when we were 16 at school, and he had this wonderful male musk smell.
lines.zh: 我和莱斯16岁时在学校相识，他身上有一种很好闻的男性麝香气息。

lines.en: He knew I was sensitive, so he didn't wear any perfume or deodorant.
lines.zh: 他知道我很敏感，所以不用香水，也不喷除臭剂。

lines.en: However, when he was 31, that smell began to change, and it worried me.
lines.zh: 然而，在他31岁时，那种气味开始发生变化，这让我很担心。

lines.en: By the time he was 33, this smell was his dominant smell.
lines.zh: 到他33岁时，这种气味已经成了他身上最明显的味道。

lines.en: But unknown to us, I had diagnosed him with a disease.
lines.zh: 但我们当时并不知道，我其实已经诊断出了他得了病。

lines.en: My nose had diagnosed him.
lines.zh: 准确地说，是我的鼻子诊断出了他的病。

lines.en: And it took medicine 12 years later to catch up, and he was diagnosed with Parkinson's disease.
lines.zh: 直到12年后，医学才终于跟上这一发现，他被确诊患上了帕金森病。

lines.en: Born with a hypersensitive nose, Joy began to recognize diseases through their scents during her decades of work as a nurse.
lines.zh: 乔伊天生嗅觉异常敏锐，在数十年的护士生涯中，她开始通过气味识别疾病。

lines.en: She shares the journey she's been on to make the best of her super smelling abilities,.
lines.zh: 她分享了自己一路走来的经历，讲述她如何尽可能发挥自己超强嗅觉的优势。

lines.en: And how her extraordinary nose has been translated into a non-invasive test, helping researchers diagnose what was right under their noses all along.
lines.zh: 以及她那非凡的嗅觉如何被转化为一种无创检测方法，帮助研究人员诊断出那个其实一直近在眼前的问题。

lines.en: That's coming up right after a short break.
lines.zh: 短暂休息之后，马上回来。

clips.title: 第一支烟和最后一支烟

lines.en: I remember my first cigarette almost as well as I remember my last.
lines.zh: 我记得我的第一支烟，几乎就像记得最后一支那样清楚。

lines.en: My first one, it was all love and honey.
lines.zh: 第一支的时候，一切都像爱情和蜜糖一样甜美。

lines.en: It tasted good.
lines.zh: 它尝起来真不错。

lines.en: It felt good on my lips.
lines.zh: 它贴在我唇边的感觉也很好。

lines.en: It melted my brain like chocolate under a hot marshmallow.
lines.zh: 它像热棉花糖底下的巧克力一样，把我的脑子都融化了。

lines.en: Last one, not so much.
lines.zh: 可最后一支，就完全不是那么回事了。

lines.en: It was one of those random summertime hailstorms, and I was huddled underneath this little overhang, smoking it with abandon, desperate need.
lines.zh: 那是夏天里一场突如其来的冰雹雨，我蜷缩在一个小小的屋檐下，放纵地抽着那支烟，带着一种近乎绝望的渴求。

lines.en: I smoked it all the way down to the filter, flicked it into this little flowerpot that, poor little thing, never saw anything beautiful.
lines.zh: 我一直抽到只剩过滤嘴，才把它弹进旁边那个小花盆里——可怜的小东西，怕是从没见过什么美好的东西。

lines.en: And I felt so frustrated with myself, so ashamed.
lines.zh: 那一刻，我对自己无比懊恼，也无比羞愧。

lines.en: I'd tried to quit plenty of times before, and there I was again.
lines.zh: 以前我已经试过很多次戒烟了，可到头来，我还是又回到了原点。

lines.en: I felt like I was letting myself down.
lines.zh: 我觉得自己辜负了自己。

lines.en: I felt like I was breaking a promise, which I was.
lines.zh: 我感觉自己违背了一个承诺，而事实也的确如此。

lines.en: I went back inside, and I went into my bedroom where I've got this big whiteboard.
lines.zh: 我回到屋里，走进了我的卧室，那里有一块很大的白板。

lines.en: It was my quitboard, and it was huge, and I only had like 60 little tallies in a corner.
lines.zh: 那是我用来记录戒掉进度的白板，很大一块，可角落里却只画了大概60个小小的记号。

clips.title: 被债务淹没的体面人生

lines.en: It happens all the time.
lines.zh: 这种事一直都在发生。

lines.en: The lovely couple with a nice house, beautiful family and gainful careers unexpectedly find themselves drowning in tens of thousands of dollars of credit card debt.
lines.zh: 那对看起来很幸福的夫妻，住着不错的房子，家庭美满，事业也稳定，却突然发现自己陷入了数万美元的信用卡债务之中。

lines.en: Debt grew by 93 billion dollars at the end of 2024 and half of that increase came from new credit card debt.
lines.zh: 到2024年底，债务增加了930亿美元，其中一半的增幅来自新增的信用卡债务。

lines.en: Now New York Federal Reserve.
lines.zh: 这是纽约联邦储备银行的数据。

lines.en: Or maybe it's the fresh college graduate who, rather than looking forward to a bright future, finds himself trying to pay back a series of compounding loans.
lines.zh: 又或者是那个刚毕业的大学生，本该期待光明的未来，却发现自己正在偿还一连串不断累积的贷款。

lines.en: Only to watch the balance grow year after year after year.
lines.zh: 只能眼睁睁看着余额一年又一年地不断增长。

lines.en: One out of every four Americans with student loans is delinquent.
lines.zh: 每四个背负学生贷款的美国人中，就有一个处于拖欠状态。

lines.en: That's nearly triple the pre-pandemic delinquency rates.
lines.zh: 这几乎是疫情前拖欠率的三倍。

lines.en: And then there's the retiree who thought she had enough saved until one medical emergency wiped out her nest egg.
lines.zh: 还有那些退休人士，本以为自己的积蓄已经足够，结果一场突如其来的医疗紧急情况就把养老钱掏空了。

lines.en: And so you just start chipping away at your savings, chipping away at everything you have and ultimately chipping away at retirement.
lines.zh: 于是你只能一点点动用积蓄，一点点耗掉自己拥有的一切，最终连退休生活也被一点点蚕食。

lines.en: It's easy to believe this would never happen to me.
lines.zh: 人很容易觉得，这种事绝不会发生在自己身上。

lines.en: I would be smarter, savvier, better at saving.
lines.zh: 我本来可以更精明、更老练，也更会存钱。

lines.en: And yet hundreds of millions of people around the world find themselves enmeshed in debt.
lines.zh: 然而，世界各地仍有数以亿计的人深陷债务之中。

lines.en: Some of this has to do with reckless spending of course.
lines.zh: 当然，这其中一部分原因确实与不计后果的消费有关。

lines.en: But even when you do everything right, budget, plan and make reasonable choices,.
lines.zh: 但即使你把该做的都做对了，认真做预算、提前规划，并作出合理的选择，

clips.title: 他咬了一口，吐了出来

lines.en: He heard that the Hershey Company had released a new Reese's chocolate.
lines.zh: 他听说好时公司推出了一款新的 Reese's 巧克力。

lines.en: It was Reese's peanut butter mini hearts unwrapped.
lines.zh: 那是一款没独立包装的 Reese's 迷你花生酱爱心巧克力。

lines.en: OK, and of course, then I went out and bought one, bought a pouch.
lines.zh: 好吧，结果我当然还是跑去买了，买了一袋。

lines.en: Brad opens the bag and pops some mini hearts in his mouth.
lines.zh: 布拉德打开袋子，往嘴里丢了几颗迷你爱心巧克力。

lines.en: And I took two bites and it was not recognizable.
lines.zh: 我咬了两口，就发现这味道完全不对。

lines.en: It was just nasty.
lines.zh: 简直难吃。

lines.en: It was not edible.
lines.zh: 根本没法吃。

lines.en: Brad spits out the mini hearts and then he dumps them in the trash.
lines.zh: 布拉德把迷你爱心巧克力吐了出来，然后全扔进了垃圾桶。

lines.en: But then he's like, wait, what was so off about those chocolate hearts?
lines.zh: 但接着他又想，等等，这些巧克力爱心到底哪里不对劲？

lines.en: I retrieved the pouch wrapper and looked carefully at the front and the back.
lines.zh: 我把那只包装袋捡了回来，仔细看了看正面和背面的说明。

lines.en: And there was no milk chocolate and there was no real peanut butter.
lines.zh: 而且里面没有牛奶巧克力，也没有真正的花生酱。

lines.en: So it wasn't even, I don't know what I, Greg, I have no idea what I was eating or what I was tasting.
lines.zh: 所以那东西根本算不上什么，我都不知道自己吃的到底是什么、尝到的又是什么，Greg，我完全搞不清。

lines.en: Brad is like, what is even in here?
lines.zh: Brad 就说，这里面到底装的是什么啊？

lines.en: No milk chocolate, no peanut butter in a chocolate Reese's peanut butter cop light product?
lines.zh: 一个号称是 Reese's 花生酱巧克力杯的产品，居然没有牛奶巧克力，也没有花生酱？

lines.en: I went to the store and just investigated everything I could.
lines.zh: 我去了商店，把能查的东西都查了一遍。

lines.en: Brad starts scanning the candy aisles, looking at the wrappers of other Reese's and Hershey's products.
lines.zh: Brad 开始扫视糖果货架，查看其他 Reese's 和 Hershey's 产品的包装。

lines.en: Are you familiar with the Reese's fast break? I am.
lines.zh: 你知道 Reese's Fast Break 吗？我知道。

lines.en: OK, one of my favorites.
lines.zh: 好，那是我最喜欢的之一。

lines.en: No longer milk chocolate. Wow.
lines.zh: 不再是牛奶巧克力了。哇。

lines.en: The Reese's sticks.
lines.zh: Reese's 那种夹心威化棒。

lines.en: Have you had those?
lines.zh: 你吃过那个吗？

lines.en: I have not had the Reese's sticks.
lines.zh: 我还没吃过 Reese's 的威化棒。

lines.en: They're similar to a Kit Kat, but peanut butter.
lines.zh: 有点像 Kit Kat，不过是花生酱口味的。

lines.en: And those came out in 1998. Very good.
lines.zh: 而且那是 1998 年推出的。非常好。

lines.en: I love them.
lines.zh: 我很喜欢。

lines.en: And it came out as the crisp you can't resist.
lines.zh: 当时的宣传语是“让人无法抗拒的酥脆口感”。

clips.title: 1928年奥运会，女性第一次站上跑道

source.episode: Tested (Ep 2): Questions of a Physical Nature

lines.en: Let's begin in the summer of 1928.
lines.zh: 让我们从1928年的夏天说起。

lines.en: Almost a hundred years ago now.
lines.zh: 那已经是将近一百年前的事了。

lines.en: That August, the Olympics was held in Amsterdam, and almost every day the Olympic Stadium was packed with fans.
lines.zh: 那年8月，奥运会在阿姆斯特丹举行，几乎每天奥林匹克体育场都挤满了观众。

lines.en: And those fans were watching something historic.
lines.zh: 而这些观众正在见证一项历史性时刻。

lines.en: This was the first Olympics where women were allowed to compete in track and field.
lines.zh: 这是奥运会历史上第一次允许女性参加田径比赛。

lines.en: Women had been allowed in the Olympics before 1928.
lines.zh: 其实在1928年之前，女性就已经被允许参加奥运会了。

lines.en: They could play tennis or swim, sports that were considered delicate and feminine.
lines.zh: 她们可以打网球或游泳，这些项目被认为更轻柔、更符合女性气质。

lines.en: But track and field had always been completely off limits.
lines.zh: 但田径项目一直是完全禁止女性参加的。

lines.en: And on August 2nd, a particularly exciting race happened.
lines.zh: 而在8月2日这天，一场格外精彩的比赛上演了。

lines.en: The 800 meters. Two laps.
lines.zh: 女子800米。两圈。

lines.en: Half a mile.
lines.zh: 半英里。

lines.en: This was the longest distance women were allowed to run.
lines.zh: 这是当时女性被允许参加跑步的最长距离。

lines.en: There is some silent footage from the race that still survives.
lines.zh: 那场比赛的一些无声影像至今仍然保存了下来。

lines.en: And as the camera pans across the crowd, you can see fans cheering, waving hats, and leaning over.
lines.zh: 当镜头扫过人群时，你可以看到观众在欢呼、挥舞帽子，还纷纷探身向前。

clips.title: 波本酒局内幕

lines.en: Okay, this show has economics in its name, so let's start with an economist.
lines.zh: 好，这档节目名字里有“经济学”，那我们就先从一位经济学家说起。

lines.en: My name is Ken Trosky.
lines.zh: 我叫肯·特罗斯基。

lines.en: I'm a labor economist and chair of the economics department at the University of Kentucky.
lines.zh: 我是劳动经济学家，也是肯塔基大学经济系主任。

lines.en: Trosky grew up in Washington state, got his PhD at the University of Chicago,.
lines.zh: 特罗斯基在华盛顿州长大，在芝加哥大学拿到博士学位，

lines.en: And he moved to Kentucky about 20 years ago. 95 of bourbon made in the world is made within a 45 minute drive of the house I live in.
lines.zh: 大约20年前搬到了肯塔基。我家方圆45分钟车程内，生产了全世界95%的波本威士忌。

lines.en: It was Trosky we heard from a minute ago saying that there are 16 million barrels of bourbon aging in Kentucky.
lines.zh: 刚才我们听到的那位特罗斯基还提到，肯塔基州有1600万桶波本威士忌正在陈酿。

lines.en: That is up from 4 million when Trosky arrived.
lines.zh: 而在特罗斯基刚到那里的时候，这个数字只有400万桶。

lines.en: When I got there, bourbon was just starting to recover from a long period of time of when it was in decline.
lines.zh: 我刚到那儿时，波本威士忌正开始从长期低迷中慢慢恢复。

lines.en: And I was hearing about this bourbon called Pappy Van Winkles.
lines.zh: 那时候我一直听人提起一种叫帕比·范温克尔的波本。

lines.en: I told my wife one time for Christmas, I said, you know, I'd like to try some.
lines.zh: 有一年圣诞节，我跟我妻子说，你知道，我想尝尝那个。

lines.en: So she went into the local big box liquor store, and there were a couple of bottles of 20 year old Pappy,.
lines.zh: 于是她去了当地一家大型连锁酒类商店，看到那儿有几瓶20年陈的帕比。

lines.en: But it was 120 a bottle, which is maybe triple what a typical bottle would be.
lines.zh: 但一瓶要120美元，差不多是普通一瓶价格的三倍。

lines.en: Oh yeah, at least.
lines.zh: 哦对，至少是这样。

lines.en: OK, it's 120.
lines.zh: 好吧，120美元。

lines.en: So she thinks it's not worth it.
lines.zh: 所以她觉得不值。

lines.en: And then she goes home, and then she says, well, it's a Christmas present. Yada yada.
lines.zh: 然后她回家了，接着又说，嗯，这毕竟是圣诞礼物，诸如此类的。

lines.en: And she goes back and she buys it at 120 a bottle.
lines.zh: 然后她又回去，以120美元一瓶的价格把它买了。

lines.en: You try to find a bottle of 20 year old Pappy's right now, and it's probably twenty five hundred, three thousand.
lines.zh: 你现在去找一瓶20年陈的帕比试试，价格大概得两千五到三千美元。

lines.en: And I'm like, why didn't you get two?
lines.zh: 我当时就想，你怎么不买两瓶呢？

lines.en: Pappy Van Winkle is one of a couple dozen bourbon brands controlled by Sazerac.
lines.zh: 帕比·范·温克尔是由萨泽拉克控制的二十多个波本威士忌品牌之一。

clips.title: 11岁那年的嫉妒

lines.en: When Sarah was a kid, the number of movie stars who came to stay at their house was exactly one, and it was kind of a disaster.
lines.zh: 萨拉小时候，来他们家借住过的电影明星一共就只有一位，而且那次经历还挺糟糕的。

lines.en: Robert Redford ended up at their house because he had heard about a book that Sarah's stepfather was writing about Leonard Peltier of the American Indian Movement.
lines.zh: 罗伯特·雷德福会来到他们家，是因为他听说萨拉的继父正在写一本关于“美国印第安人运动”成员伦纳德·佩尔蒂埃的书。

lines.en: It was still just a manuscript, and the stepfather didn't want to send copies of it around,.
lines.zh: 那本书当时还只是手稿，继父不想把副本到处分发，

lines.en: So he told Redford that the only way that Redford could read it is if he would do it at their house in Long Island. Redford agreed.
lines.zh: 所以他告诉雷德福，如果雷德福想读，就只能到他们位于长岛的家里来看。雷德福答应了。

lines.en: Sarah says the atmosphere in the house when he arrived was completely different from any other time in her childhood.
lines.zh: 萨拉说，他到来的那天，家里的气氛和她童年里任何其他时候都完全不一样。

lines.en: I remember mostly my mother consciously trying to be very charming, and being very charming, and talking to him a lot, and asking all sorts of questions,.
lines.zh: 我记得最清楚的是，我母亲明显在刻意表现得很有魅力，而且她也确实很有魅力；她跟他说了很多话，问了各种各样的问题，

lines.en: And laughing a lot at what he said, and kind of flitting about the house in a way that I hadn't remembered her doing before.
lines.zh: 还总是对他说的话笑个不停，在屋子里轻快地来回穿梭，那样子是我以前不记得她有过的。

lines.en: When Robert Redford told stories, even the simplest story about his trip to the house,.
lines.zh: 罗伯特·雷德福讲故事的时候，哪怕只是讲一个再简单不过的、关于他来这家的路上经历的小故事，

lines.en: Her parents nodded and smiled along with an enthusiasm that the stories did not necessarily seem to merit to 11 year old Sarah.
lines.zh: 她的父母都会频频点头、满脸笑容，热情得仿佛那些故事远比11岁的萨拉觉得的更值得那样对待。

clips.title: 100年前的怀表，怎么变成今天的美国制造腕表？

lines.en: Yeah. So Vortec watch company is like Vortex and Tik Tok, a beautiful clash of old and new.
lines.zh: 对。Vortic 这家手表公司这个名字，有点像把 Vortex 和 TikTok 结合在一起，是一种新旧交融的巧妙碰撞。

lines.en: And what we do is we actually take antique American pocket watches that were made a hundred years ago, and we turn them into one of a kind wristwatches.
lines.zh: 我们做的事情其实是把一百年前制造的美国古董怀表，改造成独一无二的腕表。

lines.en: So everything inside the watch is, you know,.
lines.zh: 所以这块表里面的所有东西，怎么说呢，

lines.en: Roughly a hundred years old and original American antique made by one of the great American watch companies like Hamilton, Elgin, Waltham or Illinois originally as a pocket watch.
lines.zh: 大约都有一百年历史了，而且都是美国原装古董，最初是由汉密尔顿、埃尔金、沃尔瑟姆或伊利诺伊等美国顶级制表公司制造的怀表。

lines.en: Fast forward to today, most pocket watches are scrapped for the gold and silver of their original case, leaving the inside as trash.
lines.zh: 到了今天，大多数怀表都会因为原表壳里的金银而被拆解报废，里面的机芯反而被当成垃圾丢掉。

lines.en: And we upcycle those, professionally restore them, meticulously rework those movements is what they're called inside the pocket watch.
lines.zh: 而我们会把这些东西升级再造，进行专业修复，并且精细地重新处理那些机芯——怀表内部的部件就是这么称呼的。

lines.en: And then we have a manufacturing business here where we're, we're making all the outside of the wristwatch,.
lines.zh: 然后我们在这里还有自己的制造业务，负责制作这款腕表外部的所有部分，

lines.en: The case, the crown, the hardware, the leather to turn that old pocket watch into a wristwatch.
lines.zh: 至于表壳、表冠、五金配件，还有把那块老怀表改成腕表所用的皮革。

lines.en: And it's one of the only truly American made wristwatches.
lines.zh: 而且这也是少数真正美国制造的腕表之一。

lines.en: It's a hundred percent made in USA built right here in Fort Collins, Colorado.
lines.zh: 百分之百美国制造，就在科罗拉多州柯林斯堡这儿生产组装。

lines.en: And it's a piece of American history on the wrist.
lines.zh: 戴在手上的，就是一段美国历史。

lines.en: So that's why I like to talk about the American dream because, you know,.
lines.zh: 所以这就是为什么我喜欢谈“美国梦”，因为你知道，

lines.en: I get to live the dream, running a manufacturing company, making American jobs and making stuff here.
lines.zh: 我正在实现这个梦想：经营一家制造企业，在美国创造就业机会，并且在这里生产产品。

lines.en: But you know, really that's what we do is we tell stories of history in these pocket watches turned into wristwatches. So interesting.
lines.zh: 但说到底，我们真正做的，是通过这些由怀表改造成的腕表来讲述历史故事。真的很有意思。

clips.title: 没人要的老怀表，为什么成了他们的宝藏？

source.episode: 719: Reviving the American Dream: The st

lines.en: Yeah. So if you've ever seen like American pickers on the History Channel, it's, it's kind of like that.
lines.zh: 对。所以如果你看过历史频道那个《American Pickers》之类的节目，大概就是那种感觉。

lines.en: You know, there's every day somewhere in the U.S.
lines.zh: 你知道，在美国几乎每天都会有地方

lines.en: A pawn shop, a jewelry store,.
lines.zh: 当铺、珠宝店之类的。

lines.en: A watchmaker goes out of business or somebody passes away.
lines.zh: 有时是钟表匠倒闭了，或者有人去世了。

lines.en: And they don't know what to do with all of the watches and diamonds and jewelry and all the things from that store.
lines.zh: 他们不知道该拿店里那些手表、钻石、珠宝以及其他东西怎么办。

lines.en: And a state buyers come in and they buy the entire state or, you know, they buy out the whole business and the diamonds go somewhere.
lines.zh: 这时候遗产收购商就会进来，把整批遗产都买下来，或者说把整个店盘下来，然后这些钻石之类的东西就会流向别处。

lines.en: The gold goes somewhere, you know, all the jewelry and rings go somewhere else.
lines.zh: 黄金会去另一个地方，你知道，其他珠宝和戒指也会流到别处。

lines.en: The watches, you know, can get sold.
lines.zh: 这些表嘛，你懂的，是能卖掉的。

lines.en: There's a huge market for vintage watches, especially Rolex and Omega and all those things.
lines.zh: 老式手表市场很大，尤其是劳力士、欧米茄这些。

lines.en: But there's almost no market for an antique American pocket watch.
lines.zh: 但古董美国产怀表基本没什么市场。

lines.en: Over 150 million pocket watches were made in USA between about 1850 and 1950.
lines.zh: 大概在1850年到1950年之间，美国一共造了超过1.5亿只怀表。

lines.en: So there's a lot of them out there.
lines.zh: 所以现在外面存量特别多。

lines.en: And most of them are sitting in a drawer in somebody's house.
lines.zh: 而且大多数都躺在别人家里的抽屉里。

lines.en: You know, it's grandpa's watch and it's just sitting there and nobody knows what to do with it.
lines.zh: 你知道，就是爷爷留下的那块表，放在那里，谁也不知道该怎么处理。

lines.en: Or it's in the bucket in the back of a pawn shop, and it's been scrapped for the gold or silver on the outside.
lines.zh: 又或者，它被扔在当铺后面桶里，外面的金或银已经被当作废料回收了。

lines.en: And they feel bad throwing it away cause they know it's a piece of history.
lines.zh: 他们又不忍心把它扔掉，因为他们知道那是一段历史的见证。

lines.en: It's a little engine that tells time that was made in Chicago a hundred years ago.
lines.zh: 这是个报时的小机器，一百年前在芝加哥造的。

lines.en: Um, and so they feel bad throwing it away, but there's really no use for it.
lines.zh: 嗯，所以他们也不忍心直接扔掉，但其实留着也没什么用。

lines.en: And so from those estate buys, you know, those estate buyers go out and they buy and they liquidate all of those stores,.
lines.zh: 所以通过这类遗产收购，你知道，那些遗产收购商会出去收货，然后把那些店里的东西统统清仓变现。

lines.en: They bring all those pocket watches and other things that are left over that people don't really have a use for to estate auctions.
lines.zh: 他们就会把那些怀表和其他剩下的、大家其实不太用得上的东西拿到遗产拍卖会上去卖。

lines.en: And I work with estate auctions to basically buy thousands of pocket watches and try to give them a second shot at life.
lines.zh: 而我会和遗产拍卖机构合作，基本上一次买下成千上万只怀表，尽量给它们第二次生命。

lines.en: We cherry pick the best ones that we can.
lines.zh: 我们会尽量把最好的那些挑出来。

lines.en: We bring them here to Fort Collins, Colorado, and my team of watchmakers goes through and restores just the best of the best of them.
lines.zh: 我们把它们带到科罗拉多州的柯林斯堡，然后由我的钟表匠团队逐一检查修复，只挑其中最好的精品来恢复。

lines.en: And then we turn them into a Vortec watch.
lines.zh: 然后我们把它们做成一块 Vortec 手表。

clips.title: 独立书店如何决定哪些书能上架？
clips.info_takeaway: 独立书店的书籍采购是由书店买手决定的，他们需在有限空间和薄利之间权衡，决定哪些书上架、数量多少，这直接影响书籍的商业命运。

lines.en: For most of my life, whenever I walked into my local bookstore, I never gave much of a
lines.zh: 大半辈子里，每次走进我家附近的书店，我都没怎么在意

lines.en: thought to how that day's particular assortment of books actually managed to make it there.
lines.zh: 当天那些书是怎么摆到那儿的。

lines.en: I didn't suspect for a moment that for every new book on display, there might be literally
lines.zh: 我从没想到，每一本新书背后可能

lines.en: thousands of others that had been passed over and left out in the cold.
lines.zh: 有成千上万本被忽略、没人理的书。

lines.en: Until one morning this January.
lines.zh: 直到今年一月的某个早晨。

lines.en: That's when I found myself walking into a small,
lines.zh: 那天我走进一家小小的

lines.en: independent bookstore called Carmichael's in Louisville, Kentucky.
lines.zh: 位于肯塔基州路易斯维尔的独立书店，叫 Carmichael’s。

lines.en: I was there to meet a bookseller named Fisher Nash.
lines.zh: 我是去见一位叫 Fisher Nash 的书商。

lines.en: That is me.
lines.zh: 那就是我。

lines.en: I am Fisher the bookseller.
lines.zh: 我是书商 Fisher。

lines.en: Fisher greets me wearing an all green getup.
lines.zh: Fisher 穿着一身绿色来跟我打招呼。

lines.en: Green jumpsuit, socks, shoes.
lines.zh: 绿色连体衣、袜子、鞋子。

lines.en: I wore green today in honor of Planet Money.
lines.zh: 我今天也穿了绿色，向 Planet Money 致敬。

lines.en: We love that.
lines.zh: 我们喜欢这样。

lines.en: Fisher lives and breathes books.
lines.zh: Fisher 对书简直是入骨的热爱。

lines.en: If you were a character in a novel, how would you describe yourself?
lines.zh: 如果你是小说里的角色，你会怎么形容自己？

lines.en: Oh, wow.
lines.zh: 哇哦。

lines.en: That's a really hard question.
lines.zh: 这问题真难。

lines.en: I would probably say I am a quirky bookseller whose personality is mainly books.
lines.zh: 我大概会说我是个古怪的书商，性格基本就是书。

lines.en: How do you feel about You've Got Mail?
lines.zh: 你对《电子情书》有什么感觉？

lines.en: Oh, I love You've Got Mail, the Meg Ryan movie from the 90s.
lines.zh: 哦，我超喜欢《电子情书》，就是90年代梅格·瑞恩演的那部。

lines.en: It's so good.
lines.zh: 特别好看。

lines.en: It's my favorite bookstore movie.
lines.zh: 这是我最喜欢的书店题材电影。

lines.en: And in their job at Carmichael's, Fisher holds one of the greatest responsibilities
lines.zh: 在卡迈克尔书店的工作里，费舍尔肩负着最重要的职责之一

lines.en: in the whole publishing ecosystem.
lines.zh: 在整个出版圈里都是。

lines.en: Who decides what books end up in bookstores?
lines.zh: 谁来决定哪些书能进书店？

lines.en: That is me.
lines.zh: 那就是我。

lines.en: It is primarily me.
lines.zh: 主要就是我。

lines.en: I am the book buyer.
lines.zh: 我是书籍采购员。

lines.en: The book buyer is a specific role at basically every bookstore or book chain around the country.
lines.zh: 书籍采购员是全国几乎每家书店或连锁书店都会有的专门职位。

lines.en: At Carmichael's bookstore, Fisher is the book buyer.
lines.zh: 在卡迈克尔书店，费舍尔是负责进书的人。

lines.en: So I am the one who meets with sales reps from publishers, and I'm the one who decides
lines.zh: 所以我就是那个跟出版社销售代表见面的人，也是做决定的人。

lines.en: which new books we're going to bring in, how many, and where they will go.
lines.zh: 决定要进哪些新书、进多少、放在哪。

lines.en: Book buyers like Fisher will be deciding the commercial fate of the Planet Money book.
lines.zh: 像费舍尔这样的进书人会决定《Planet Money》这本书的商业命运。

lines.en: And so I wanted to understand how these decisions actually get made.
lines.zh: 所以我想弄清楚这些决定到底是怎么做的。

lines.en: Now, running an independent bookstore, Fisher explains, is a game of thin margins.
lines.zh: 费舍尔说，经营独立书店就是在薄利中求生存。

lines.en: Order too few copies of a popular new book,
lines.zh: 热门新书进货太少，

lines.en: and you'll be depriving your store of much needed financial lifeblood.
lines.zh: 就会让书店失去急需的资金来源。

lines.en: Order too many, and you'll be clogging up coveted shelf space that could be holding
lines.zh: 进货太多，就会占掉宝贵的书架空间，本来可以放别的书。

lines.zh: 谢谢。

options: A. The astronauts traveled farther from Earth than anyone ever had.
B. The astronauts landed on the moon for the first time.
C. The astronauts stayed in contact with Earth throughout the mission.
D. The astronauts discovered new planets.
questions.explanation_zh: 文章明确指出他们到达了比任何人以前更远离地球的距离。

questions.question: During the Artemis II mission, what happened when the astronauts flew behind the moon?
options: A. They were completely out of contact with Earth for about 40 minutes.
B. They received continuous messages from Houston.
C. They landed on the moon's far side.
D. They saw Earth disappear permanently.
questions.explanation_zh: 文章提到他们飞到月球背面时与地球完全失去联系约40分钟。

clips.title: 为什么债务陷阱比你想象的更近
clips.info_takeaway: 债务陷阱不仅源于不理性消费，即使合理规划也可能因突发事件或结构性因素而陷入困境。

lines.en: unexpectedly find themselves drowning in tens of thousands of dollars of credit card debt.
lines.zh: 意外陷入几万美元的信用卡债务里。

lines.en: Debt grew by 93 billion dollars at the end of 2024
lines.zh: 到2024年底，债务增加了930亿美元。

lines.en: and half of that increase came from new credit card debt.
lines.zh: 其中一半是新的信用卡债务。

lines.en: Now New York Federal Reserve.
lines.zh: 现在是纽约联邦储备局的消息。

lines.en: Or maybe it's the fresh college graduate who rather than looking forward to a bright future
lines.zh: 或者是刚毕业的大学生，本该期待光明的未来。

lines.en: and a sense of freedom finds himself trying to pay back a series of compounding loans
lines.zh: 却不得不拼命偿还一堆滚雪球般的贷款。

lines.en: only to watch the balance grow year after year after year.
lines.zh: 结果每年都看着欠款越积越多。

lines.en: One out of every four Americans with student loans
lines.zh: 每四个有学生贷款的美国人里就有一个

lines.en: is delinquent.
lines.zh: 拖欠还款。

lines.en: That's nearly triple the pre-pandemic delinquency rates.
lines.zh: 这个比例几乎是疫情前的三倍。

lines.en: And then there's the retiree who thought she had enough saved
lines.zh: 还有那位退休的人，本以为存的钱够用了

lines.en: until one medical emergency wiped out her nest egg.
lines.zh: 结果一次突发的医疗事故把她的积蓄全花光了

lines.en: And so you just start chipping away at your savings,
lines.zh: 于是你就开始一点点动用存款

lines.en: chipping away at everything you have and ultimately chipping away at retirement.
lines.zh: 一点点耗掉所有的资产，最后连退休都被耗掉了

lines.en: It's easy to believe this would never happen to me.
lines.zh: 很容易觉得这种事不会发生在我身上

lines.en: I would be smarter, savvier, better at saving.
lines.zh: 我会更聪明、更精明、更会存钱

questions.question: According to the passage, what portion of the 93 billion dollar debt increase at the end of 2024 came from new credit card debt?
options: A. Half of it
B. All of it
C. One quarter of it
D. None of it
questions.explanation_zh: 文中明确指出93亿美元债务增长的一半来自新的信用卡债务。

questions.question: What happened to the retiree mentioned in the passage?
options: A. A medical emergency depleted her savings
B. She invested all her money in stocks
C. She received unexpected inheritance
D. She decided to return to work
questions.explanation_zh: 文中描述退休者因一次医疗紧急情况耗尽了储蓄。

clips.title: AI其实只是数学，但它已无处不在
clips.info_takeaway: AI本质上是数学模型，通过数据学习来预测，并已深度融入我们生活的方方面面。

source.episode: Will AI destroy us... or save us?

lines.en: What does AI actually mean, you know?
lines.zh: 你知道，AI到底是什么意思？

lines.en: Really mean?
lines.zh: 真正的意思是什么？

lines.en: AI is not a magic wand, but it's not a Terminator.
lines.zh: AI不是魔法棒，也不是终结者。

lines.en: This system learns by accessing data.
lines.zh: 这个系统是通过访问数据来学习的。

lines.en: AI is not a harbinger of utopia or dystopia.
lines.zh: AI既不是乌托邦的预兆，也不是反乌托邦的预兆。

lines.en: And it changes its configuration in order to be able to predict stuff.
lines.zh: 它会调整自己的配置来预测东西。

lines.en: AI is something invented by us to advance our progress.
lines.zh: AI是我们发明出来推动进步的东西。

lines.en: It's really complicated, beautiful math,
lines.zh: 它是很复杂又漂亮的数学，

lines.en: but it is fundamentally just math.
lines.zh: 但归根到底就是数学。

lines.en: AI is deeply entangled with our desire to be in control,
lines.zh: AI和我们想掌控一切的欲望紧密相连。

lines.en: both of ourselves and the world around us.
lines.zh: 我们自己和周围的世界

lines.en: To control human behavior, to control the future,
lines.zh: 控制人的行为，掌握未来

lines.en: to control environmental outcomes,
lines.zh: 控制环境的结果

lines.en: to control institutions and societies.
lines.zh: 控制机构和社会

lines.en: AI is already here.
lines.zh: 人工智能已经来了

lines.en: It's in your phones.
lines.zh: 它就在你的手机里

lines.en: Siri or Google or Alexa.
lines.zh: Siri、Google、Alexa

lines.en: Your cars.
lines.zh: 你的车

lines.en: Google Maps uses a very smart algorithm to provide...
lines.zh: 谷歌地图用很聪明的算法来提供...

lines.en: Your homes.
lines.zh: 你的家

lines.en: The home security system uses artificial intelligence
lines.zh: 家庭安防系统用上了人工智能

lines.en: to keep your family safe.
lines.zh: 来保护你家人的安全

lines.en: Your skies.
lines.zh: 你的天空

lines.en: From drones to unmanned aircrafts and beyond.
lines.zh: 从无人机到无人飞机等等

lines.en: Your hospitals.
lines.zh: 你的医院

lines.en: Interpreting x-rays.
lines.zh: 看懂X光片

lines.en: Your love lives.
lines.zh: 你的感情生活

lines.en: The dating apps.
lines.zh: 交友软件

lines.en: Your virtual lives.
lines.zh: 你的虚拟生活

lines.en: Social media companies spy on us.
lines.zh: 社交媒体公司在监视我们

lines.en: Your governments.
lines.zh: 你们的政府

lines.en: What it means for the future of our democracy.
lines.zh: 这对我们民主的未来意味着什么

lines.en: So we are, in fact, totally immersed in an AI world.
lines.zh: 所以我们其实已经完全生活在人工智能的世界里

questions.question: According to the passage, what is AI fundamentally?
options: A. Complicated mathematics
B. A magical tool
C. A dangerous robot
D. A prediction of utopia
questions.explanation_zh: 文中明确指出AI本质上是复杂的数学，因此选A。

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the passage as an example of AI in daily life?
options: A. Home security systems
B. Dating apps
C. Weather forecasting
D. Interpreting x-rays
questions.explanation_zh: 文中提到家庭安防、约会应用、解读X光，但没有提到天气预报，因此选C。

clips.title: 美国人每天吃肉是怎么被“制造”出来的
clips.info_takeaway: 二战后，美国通过政府补贴、大规模养殖技术、冷藏普及和学校午餐计划，使得每日吃肉成为常态。

source.episode: The hidden forces shaping your choices

lines.en: In mid-century America, there was a strategy to make meat the norm, starting after the Second World War.
lines.zh: 在二十世纪中期的美国，有个策略是让吃肉变成常态，从二战结束后开始推行。

lines.en: As savory beef stew when it's armored.
lines.zh: 就像加了防护的咸香牛肉炖菜。

lines.en: It was impossible to avoid and it was advertised heavily and pushed on consumers.
lines.zh: 几乎避不开，到处打广告，拼命推给消费者。

lines.en: Hormel bacon stays fresh longer when saran wrapped.
lines.zh: 荷美尔的培根用保鲜膜包起来能放得更久。

lines.en: This is when they first started creating national ad campaigns.
lines.zh: 那时候他们才开始做全国性的广告宣传。

lines.en: And when you see these ads that meat is essential for your amino acids.
lines.zh: 你会看到这些广告说肉对你的氨基酸很重要。

lines.en: It's essential for a well-balanced diet.
lines.zh: 它是均衡饮食不可缺的。

lines.en: Found that man must have food which furnishes energy.
lines.zh: 发现人必须吃能提供能量的食物。

lines.en: Food to build muscle.
lines.zh: 能让肌肉增长的食物。

lines.en: And this is where we started getting this idea that meat was normal to eat every day in multiple meals.
lines.zh: 就是从这时候开始，我们觉得每天多顿吃肉是很正常的。

lines.en: When in reality, even ten years prior to that, it was an exception to have meat at a meal rather than the norm.
lines.zh: 其实在那之前十年，饭桌上有肉还是例外，不是常态。

lines.en: But three factors completely flipped what was for dinner.
lines.zh: 但有三个因素彻底改变了餐桌上的菜。

lines.en: First, the U.S.
lines.zh: 第一，美国

lines.en: government increased its meat subsidies.
lines.zh: 政府加大了对肉类的补贴。

lines.en: Second, new technology led to larger farms that could raise and slaughter more animals faster.
lines.zh: 第二，新技术让农场规模更大，养殖和屠宰速度更快。

lines.en: Plus, people could refrigerate and store more meat.
lines.zh: 而且人们可以冷藏、储存更多的肉。

lines.en: Finally, the National School Lunch Program launched.
lines.zh: 最后，全国学校午餐计划启动了。

lines.en: Lunches are designed to be nutritious.
lines.zh: 午餐是按营养来设计的。

lines.en: With a mandate to serve protein.
lines.zh: 规定必须有蛋白质。

lines.en: Meats and other foods rich in protein.
lines.zh: 比如肉类和其他高蛋白的食物。

lines.en: We saw the fastest and largest shift in our diets that has ever really happened.
lines.zh: 我们经历了史上最快、最大的一次饮食变化。

questions.question: What was one factor that contributed to making meat a daily norm in mid-century America?
options: A. The U.S. government increased its meat subsidies
B. People began importing more meat from overseas
C. Restaurants stopped serving vegetarian meals
D. Farmers switched entirely to poultry production
questions.explanation_zh: 文章提到政府增加肉类补贴是导致肉类成为日常饮食的重要因素之一。

questions.question: According to the passage, how were meats promoted to consumers after the Second World War?
options: A. Through national advertising campaigns emphasizing their nutritional value
B. By lowering prices through international trade agreements
C. By banning alternative protein sources
D. Through mandatory meat consumption laws
questions.explanation_zh: 文章明确指出在战后首次开展全国广告活动，强调肉类对氨基酸和均衡饮食的重要性。

clips.title: 一家人意外见证首颗原子弹试爆
clips.info_takeaway: 用户能了解到1945年美国首次原子弹试验（Trinity Test）的时间、地点，以及一个普通家庭偶然见证这一历史时刻的经历。

source.episode: Bright as Daybreak, Red as Fire

lines.en: My great-grandmother and great-grandfather,
lines.zh: 我的曾祖母和曾祖父

lines.en: they were ranchers in Southern New Mexico
lines.zh: 他们是在新墨西哥州南部的牧场主

lines.en: and they lived there their entire lives
lines.zh: 一辈子都住在那里

lines.en: out near the town of Las Cruces.
lines.zh: 在拉斯克鲁塞斯镇附近

lines.en: Tyler Johnson called our voicemail line
lines.zh: 泰勒·约翰逊打电话到我们的语音信箱

lines.en: to share a moment where his family members
lines.zh: 想分享他家人经历的一个瞬间

lines.en: had an accidental brush with history.
lines.zh: 他们无意间和历史擦肩而过

lines.en: They told us that in 1945,
lines.zh: 他们跟我们说在1945年

lines.en: they had gotten in the family car
lines.zh: 他们开着家里的车

lines.en: and driven to a town near the border of Arizona
lines.zh: 去了亚利桑那州边境附近的一个小镇

lines.en: and they had to stop for the night,
lines.zh: 他们不得不停下来过夜

lines.en: so they slept in their car on the side of the road.
lines.zh: 于是就在路边的车里睡了

lines.en: Early the next morning,
lines.zh: 第二天一大早

lines.en: they were awoken by a bright flash.
lines.zh: 他们被一道强光惊醒

lines.en: And that bright flash turned out to be the Trinity test,
lines.zh: 那道强光其实是“三位一体”核试验

lines.en: the first atomic bomb ever tested.
lines.zh: 这是第一次测试原子弹

lines.en: And they just happened to witness it in their car.
lines.zh: 他们就这么在车里碰巧看到了

lines.en: It was less than a month after the Trinity test
lines.zh: 不到一个月后

lines.en: that the U.S.
lines.zh: 美国

lines.en: dropped atomic bombs
lines.zh: 投下了原子弹

lines.en: on Hiroshima and Nagasaki.
lines.zh: 在广岛和长崎。

lines.en: That was the start of the atomic age
lines.zh: 那是原子时代的开始

lines.en: that continues to this day.
lines.zh: 一直延续到今天。

questions.question: Where did Tyler Johnson's great-grandparents live their entire lives?
options: A. Near the town of Las Cruces in Southern New Mexico
B. Near the border of Arizona
C. In Hiroshima, Japan
D. In Northern New Mexico
questions.explanation_zh: 文中明确提到他们一生都住在南新墨西哥的拉斯克鲁塞斯镇附近。

questions.question: What was the bright flash that Tyler Johnson's great-grandparents witnessed?
options: A. The Trinity test, the first atomic bomb ever tested
B. A meteor shower
C. A sunrise
D. A fireworks display
questions.explanation_zh: 文中指出那道亮光是“三位一体”试验，即第一次原子弹测试。

clips.title: 冷战少年偷看机密地图的恐惧记忆
clips.info_takeaway: 用户能了解到冷战时期美国民众对核战争的恐惧，包括苏联攻击目标地图和核攻击时间预估等细节。

source.episode: Bright as Daybreak, Red as Fire

lines.en: When I really became conscious of the Cold War
lines.zh: 我真正意识到冷战的时候

lines.en: was in eighth grade.
lines.zh: 是在八年级。

lines.en: My dad was in the military and he had an office at home.
lines.zh: 我爸是军人，在家里有个办公室。

lines.en: That's John Conmey telling his kids what it was like
lines.zh: 这是约翰·康米在跟孩子们讲当时的情况

lines.en: growing up near Washington, D.C.
lines.zh: 他是在华盛顿特区附近长大的

lines.en: in the 60s.
lines.zh: 六十年代的事。

lines.en: We weren't supposed to be going in the office,
lines.zh: 我们本来不该进那个办公室

lines.en: but I was very curious.
lines.zh: 但我特别好奇。

lines.en: And on his desk was a classified document,
lines.zh: 他桌上放着一份机密文件

lines.en: a series of maps where the Soviets,
lines.zh: 是一系列苏联的地图

lines.en: if they bombed us, the targets they were going to hit.
lines.zh: 如果他们轰炸我们，那些他们要打的目标。

lines.en: I knew that if an attack came
lines.zh: 我知道如果真的遭到攻击

lines.en: and it would hit Washington, D.C.,
lines.zh: 而且会打到华盛顿特区

lines.en: there'd be about 15 minutes
lines.zh: 会有大约十五分钟

lines.en: before the first fallout would come.
lines.zh: 才会有第一波辐射尘到来

lines.en: And I had actually timed how long it would take
lines.zh: 我还真算过要花多久

lines.en: to run from school all the way home.
lines.zh: 才能从学校一路跑回家

lines.en: Not until I was grown did I feel comfortable
lines.zh: 直到长大我才觉得可以放心

lines.en: talking about the fear I experienced
lines.zh: 去谈我当时的恐惧

lines.en: growing up with the Cold War.
lines.zh: 在冷战中长大的那种害怕

lines.en: People don't understand how close we came
lines.zh: 人们不知道我们差点就到了那一步

lines.en: and have war broken out.
lines.zh: 战争差点爆发

lines.en: It would have been catastrophic.
lines.zh: 那会是场灾难

lines.en: We were at true risk of mankind as a species
lines.zh: 我们真的面临人类这个物种的风险

lines.en: being annihilated.
lines.zh: 差点被彻底消灭

questions.question: What did John Conmey find on his father's desk?
options: A. A classified document with maps showing Soviet bombing targets
B. A letter from the President about the Cold War
C. A diary describing military life
D. A newspaper article about Washington, D.C.
questions.explanation_zh: 文中明确提到他在父亲的桌上看到的是一份机密文件，里面有苏联轰炸目标的地图。

questions.question: How much time did John Conmey think there would be before fallout reached Washington, D.C. after an attack?
options: A. About 15 minutes
B. About 5 minutes
C. About 30 minutes
D. About 1 hour
questions.explanation_zh: 文中提到如果攻击发生，放射性尘埃到达华盛顿大约需要15分钟。
